content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

January 17, 1996

Charles Peterson
Supervisor, Fifth District
County of Mendocino
Courthouse
Ukiah, California 95482

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-018

Dear Mr. Peterson:

This is in response to your request for advice regarding your
responsibilities as a public official with regard to the gift
limit and conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

What are the gift limit and conflict-of-interest provisions
you need to be concerned with if your friends and supporters raise
funds to buy you a car?

CONCLUSION

Any donations given by your friends and supporters to buy you
a car will be subject to the $280 a year gift limit for local
elected officials. You must disclose any donations over $50 you
receive and you must disqualify yourself from any decision which
will have a material financial effect on any donor who gives you
$280 in the 12 months prior to the decision.

________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-96-018
Page 2


FACTS

You were inaugurated as Fifth District Supervisor of
Mendocino County on January 3, 1995. You are physically disabled
from multiple muscular-skeletal birth defects and have received a
Social Security Disability Insurance ("SSDI") check for most of
your adult life. You are eligible for, but do not receive, In
Home Supportive Services through state Social Services. Pursuant
to the Americans with Disabilities Act, you spend most of your
supervisor salary to pay for three part-time personal aides who
perform a wide variety of personal and administrative tasks. As a
result, your salary is diminished to less than $500 per month and
your SSDI check is your main source of income.

Recently, friends and supporters have organized to raise
funds to purchase a new car for you which you will need in order
to perform your duties as a county supervisor. Your previous car
is no longer operational. You would like to know the restrictions
and requirements with regard to limits on gifts and the effect
these gifts will have on possible conflicts of interest.

ANALYSIS

Section 89503 provides that no elected local officeholder may
accept any gifts from a single source in a calendar year in excess
of the gift limit. The current gift limit is $280. (Regulation
18940.1.)

The definition of a gift is "any payment to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received..." 
(Regulation 18941(a).) You have indicated that friends and
supporters have gotten together to raise funds to buy you a
vehicle. A car, or money to buy a car, for an official's personal
use is considered a gift for purposes of the Act. As such, you
may not accept a donation of over $280 per year from each of the
donors who contribute to the fund to buy you a car.²

The Act also requires that every public official disclose all
of the official's economic interests that could foreseeably be
affected by the exercise of the official's duties. (Sections
81002(c), 87200-87313.) As a county supervisor for the County of
Mendocino, you are required to disclose the name and address of
each source of a gift of more than $50 received during the
previous 12 months, a general description of the business
activity, if any, of each source, the amount of the gift and the

2 Please keep in mind that the gift limit is aggregated over the
calendar year. (Section 89503(a).) Therefore, if any of the
donors has already given you any gifts in the same calendar year,
you would need to add the value of that gift to any amount the
donor may give you for the car so that the total sum of the gifts
received is $280 or less.

File No. A-96-018
Page 3


date on which the gift was received.  (Sections 87202-87203,
87207.)

Further, Sections 87100 and 87103 require you to disqualify
yourself from any decision which will have a material financial
effect on any donor of a gift of $280.  Therefore, not only will
the gift be subject to the $280 gift limit, but you must disclose
the source of the gift and disqualify yourself from decisions
which will have a material financial effect on the donor if the
value of the gifts received from the donor totals $280 during the
12 months prior to the decision.

If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        By:  Liane Randolph
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SCC:LR:ak
